key_insight: A physics-informed neural network can learn transient elastodynamic wave propagation across a bimaterial interface and act as a continuous surrogate for stress, strain, and displacement fields.
engineering_relevance: This enables fast, physics-consistent prediction of impact-wave behavior in heterogeneous solids such as Split Hopkinson Pressure Bar specimens, reducing reliance on repeated high-cost finite-element simulations.
method: Encode axisymmetric linear elasticity, initial/boundary conditions, and interface continuity into the PINN loss.
Train and validate with high-fidelity ANSYS Explicit Dynamics simulations as supplementary constraints.
Test interpolation in time and generalization to modified material properties and additional bimaterial combinations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports close agreement with finite-element solutions for wave transmission/reflection, displacement histories, face-averaged responses, and dominant stress/strain evolution, plus successful predictions at unseen time instants.
watch_item: Performance may depend on careful loss balancing and the availability of sufficiently accurate FEM/experimental constraints, especially near sharp wave fronts and interface discontinuities.
paper_title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Elastodynamic Wave Propagation in Bimaterial Systems
